summary:
The production of olive oil from the fruit of the cultivated olive tree (Olea europaea) is attested archaeologically from at least 4000 BCE in the eastern Mediterranean. Olive presses, storage vessels with olive residues, and carbonized olive pits have been identified at sites in the Levant, Crete, and the Aegean, pointing to the systematic cultivation of olives and the extraction of oil for food, lighting, cosmetics, and ritual use. The earliest large-scale olive oil production facilities identified archaeologically date to approximately 3500–3000 BCE in Israel and the southern Levant.

Olive oil became one of the foundational commodities of Mediterranean civilizations, traded across vast distances and stored in large ceramic jars (pithoi and amphorae). Its importance is reflected in the Linear B tablets of Mycenaean Greece and in Egyptian records of imported Levantine oil. The cultivation of the olive, together with wheat and grapevine, formed the agricultural triad that would sustain Mediterranean economies for millennia. The development of olive oil production in the fourth millennium BCE represents an early example of specialized cash-crop agriculture integrated into long-distance trade networks.
